mission.title: Install and Configure Aeraki Mesh on Kubernetes
mission.description: Aeraki is a service mesh that allows you to manage any layer-7 protocols, enhancing the capabilities of microservices. Installing Aeraki enables you to handle diverse traffic types beyond just HTTP, making your service mesh more versatile.

steps.title: Clone the Aeraki repository
steps.description:
Start by cloning the Aeraki repository to your local machine to access the necessary configuration files.
```bash
git clone https://github.com/aeraki-mesh/aeraki.git
cd aeraki
```

steps.title: Deploy Aeraki using kubectl
steps.description:
Apply the Aeraki manifests to your Kubernetes cluster. Ensure you are in the `aeraki` directory where the manifests are located.
```bash
kubectl apply -f deploy/namespace.yaml
kubectl apply -f deploy/aeraki.yaml --namespace aeraki-system
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that the Aeraki pods are running successfully in the `aeraki-system` namespace.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n aeraki-system
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits
steps.description:
Edit the Aeraki deployment to set resource limits for the containers. This helps ensure that the pods have the necessary resources allocated.
```bash
kubectl edit deployment aeraki -n aeraki-system
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   requests:
#     cpu: "100m"
#     memory: "128Mi"
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"
```

steps.title: Set up monitoring
steps.description: Integrate monitoring by deploying Prometheus and Grafana to visualize Aeraki metrics. Follow the specific instructions in the Aeraki documentation for setting this up.

uninstall.title: Remove the Aeraki deployment
uninstall.description:
Delete the Aeraki deployment and any associated resources in the `aeraki-system` namespace.
```bash
kubectl delete -f deploy/aeraki.yaml --namespace aeraki-system
kubectl delete namespace aeraki-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up remaining resources
uninstall.description:
Ensure that all resources related to Aeraki are removed from the cluster.
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep aeraki | awk '{print $1}')
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current Aeraki configuration and any custom resources.
```bash
kubectl get all -n aeraki-system -o yaml > aeraki-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Aeraki manifests
upgrade.description:
Pull the latest changes from the Aeraki repository to get the updated manifests.
```bash
git pull origin master
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade the Aeraki deployment
upgrade.description:
Apply the updated manifests to upgrade Aeraki in your cluster.
```bash
kubectl apply -f deploy/aeraki.yaml --namespace aeraki-system
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Aeraki pods are running the new version successfully.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n aeraki-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods not starting
troubleshooting.description:
If the Aeraki pods are not starting, check the pod status and logs for errors.
```bash
kubectl get pods -n aeraki-system
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n aeraki-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Service not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If services managed by Aeraki are not reachable, verify the service configuration and network policies.
```bash
kubectl get svc -n aeraki-system
kubectl describe svc <service-name> -n aeraki-system
```

troubleshooting.title: High resource usage
troubleshooting.description:
If Aeraki pods are consuming too many resources, check the resource limits and adjust them accordingly.
```bash
kubectl top pods -n aeraki-system
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Aeraki will result in all pods running in the `aeraki-system` namespace without errors. You will be able to manage various layer-7 protocols effectively within your service mesh.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f deploy/namespace.yaml
kubectl apply -f deploy/aeraki.yaml --namespace aeraki-system

kubectl edit deployment aeraki -n aeraki-system
# Add the following under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   requests:
#     cpu: "100m"
#     memory: "128Mi"
#   limits:
#     cpu: "500m"
#     memory: "512Mi"

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and kubectl installed on your local machine.
